thig14.1:1.1: As Subhā the nun was going through Jīvaka’s delightful mango grove,
thig14.1:1.3: a libertine (a goldsmith’s son) blocked her path, so she said to him:
thig14.1:2.2: that you stand in my way?
thig14.1:2.4: that a man should touch a woman gone forth.
thig14.1:3.2: the training pointed out by the One Well-Gone.
thig14.1:3.4: Why do you stand in my way?
thig14.1:4.4: Why do you stand in my way?’
thig14.1:5.2: what need do you have for going forth?
thig14.1:5.4: Come, let’s delight in the flowering grove.
thig14.1:6.2: the trees risen-up with their pollen.
thig14.1:6.3: The beginning of spring is a pleasant season—
thig14.1:6.4: Come, let’s delight in the flowering grove.
thig14.1:7.1: The trees with their blossoming tips
thig14.1:7.2: moan, as it were, in the breeze:
thig14.1:7.4: if you plunge into the grove alone?
thig14.1:8.1: Frequented by herds of wild beasts,
thig14.1:8.4: into the great, lonely, frightening grove?
thig14.1:9.1: Like a doll made of gold, you will go about,
thig14.1:9.2: like a goddess in the gardens of heaven.
thig14.1:9.4: you will shine, O beauty without compare.
thig14.1:10.1: I would gladly do your every bidding
thig14.1:10.2: if we were to dwell in the glade.
thig14.1:10.3: For there is no creature dearer to me
thig14.1:10.4: than you, O nymph with the languid regard.
thig14.1:11.1: If you do as I ask, happy, come live in my house.
thig14.1:11.3: Dwelling in the calm of a palace,
thig14.1:12.3: I will make you many & varied ornaments
thig14.1:13.4: spread with a woolen quilt, brand new.
thig14.1:14.1: Like a blue lotus rising from the water
thig14.1:14.3: you will go to old age with your limbs unseen,
thig14.1:14.4: if you stay as you are in the holy life.’
thig14.1:15.1: ‘What do you assume of any essence,
thig14.1:15.2: here in this cemetery grower, filled with corpses,
thig14.1:15.3: this body destined to break up?
thig14.1:15.4: What do you see when you look at me, you who are out of your mind?’
thig14.1:16.1: ‘Your eyes are like those of a fawn,
thig14.1:16.2: like those of a sprite in the mountains.
thig14.1:16.3: Seeing your eyes, my sensual delight
thig14.1:17.1: Like tips they are, of blue lotuses,
thig14.1:17.3: Seeing your eyes, my sensual delight
thig14.1:18.1: Even if you should go far away,
thig14.1:18.2: I will think only of your pure, long-lashed gaze,
thig14.1:18.3: for there is nothing dearer to me
thig14.1:18.4: than your eyes, O nymph with the languid regard.’
thig14.1:19.1: ‘You want to stray from the road,
thig14.1:19.2: you want the moon as a plaything,
thig14.1:19.3: you want to jump over Mount Sineru,
thig14.1:19.4: you who have designs on one born of the Buddha.
thig14.1:20.1: For there is nothing anywhere at all in the cosmos with its devas,
thig14.1:20.2: that would be an object of passion for me.
thig14.1:20.3: I don’t even know what that passion would be,
thig14.1:20.4: for it’s been killed, root & all, by the path.
thig14.1:21.3: I don’t even see what that passion would be,
thig14.1:21.4: for it’s been killed, root & all, by the path.
thig14.1:22.1: Try to seduce one who hasn’t reflected on this,
thig14.1:22.2: or who has not followed the Master’s teaching.
thig14.1:22.3: But try it with this one who knows
thig14.1:23.1: For in the midst of praise & blame,
thig14.1:23.2: pleasure & pain, my mindfulness stands firm.
thig14.1:23.3: Knowing the unattractiveness of things compounded,
thig14.1:23.4: my mind cleaves to nothing at all.
thig14.1:24.1: I am a follower of the One Well-Gone,
thig14.1:24.2: riding the vehicle of the eightfold way:
thig14.1:24.4: I delight, having gone to an empty dwelling.
thig14.1:25.1: For I have seen well-painted puppets,
thig14.1:25.4: made to dance in various ways.
thig14.1:26.1: When the sticks & strings are removed,
thig14.1:26.3: smashed into pieces, not to be found,
thig14.1:26.4: in what will the mind there make its home?
thig14.1:27.1: This body of mine, which is just like that,
thig14.1:27.2: when devoid of dhammas doesn’t function.
thig14.1:27.3: When, devoid of dhammas, it doesn’t function,
thig14.1:27.4: in what will the mind there make its home?
thig14.1:28.1: Like a mural you’ve seen, painted on a wall,
thig14.1:28.3: there your vision has been distorted,
thig14.1:28.4: your perception of a human being—pointless.
thig14.1:29.2: like a tree of gold in a dream,
thig14.1:29.3: like a magic show in the midst of a crowd— you run blind after what is unreal.
thig14.1:30.1: Resembling a ball of sealing wax, set in a hollow,
thig14.1:30.2: with a bubble in the middle and bathed with tears,
thig14.1:30.3: eye secretions are born there too:
thig14.1:30.4: The parts of the eye are rolled all together in various ways.’
thig14.1:31.2: with mind unattached she felt no regret.
thig14.1:31.4: Straightaway she gave it to him.
thig14.1:32.1: Straightaway his passion faded right there,
thig14.1:32.3: ‘Be well, follower of the holy life.
thig14.1:32.4: This sort of thing won’t happen again.
thig14.1:33.2: is like embracing a blazing fire.
thig14.1:33.3: It’s as if I have seized a poisonous snake.
thig14.1:33.4:
So may you be well.
Forgive me.’
thig14.1:34.1: And released from there, the nun
thig14.1:34.2: went to the excellent Buddha’s presence.
thig14.1:34.3: When she saw the mark of his excellent merit,
thig14.1:34.4: her eye became as it was before.
